topic: Demonstratives (este, ese, esto) and everyday nouns

tips.title: Esto pairs up with eso, the word you already know
tips.body: Back on Day 2 you learned eso for 'that' (neuter, no gender). Now esto is its partner: 'this' (neuter). Este and esta point at a specific masculine or feminine noun, but esto and eso point at whole ideas or unnamed things -- 'Esto es genial!' means 'this (whole situation) is great!'

tips.title: En verdad and de verdad both come from 'truth'
tips.body: Verdad means truth, and Spanish builds two handy phrases from it: en verdad (actually, in truth) and de verdad (really, truly). En verdad usually opens a sentence to correct or clarify something; de verdad often intensifies a statement or asks a question, like '¿de verdad?' (really?).

tips.title: Tiempo and vez are both 'time' in English, but not in Spanish
tips.body: Tiempo is time in general -- duration, a period, even the weather. Vez is one specific occurrence -- 'una vez' means 'one time.' English speakers often mix these up because English uses just one word for both ideas.

bonusCards.english: This day, I want to eat with this girl.
That boy and that girl are here this time.
This is not the truth; that is the truth.
Sir, is this thing for you?
Actually, this has been a good time for my friend.

resources.name: Kwiziq Spanish — Using esto, este, esta, estos, estas (demonstrative pronouns)
